sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: SPACE.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 4, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: Maze navigation puzzle. Blue square with black dot (top-left of maze) is the player. Blue square with blue dot (bottom-right) is likely the goal. Red bar is an obstacle or moving element. White corridors are the path. Black areas are walls.
- Goal model: Navigate from the starting blue square to the target blue square through white corridors.
- Action model: UP/DOWN/LEFT/RIGHT move the player through the maze.
- Open questions: How to reach the goal room which appears isolated by white corridors? Is the red bar interactive? What does SPACE do?
- Plan: Try SPACE to see if it interacts with the red bar or changes the maze.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 3 of about 78 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
SPACE teleported the player back to the starting position [8,14]-[12,18]. This confirms SPACE is a "reset" or "restart" action.
Now I need to figure out the actual path through the maze. Let me reconsider the maze structure. The player is back at [8,14]-[12,18].
Key observations:
1. Player moves 6 cells per action (from [8,14]-[12,18] to [8,20]-[12,24] with RIGHT)
2. DOWN was blocked by white corridor
3. SPACE resets to start
4. White cells are walls, black cells are walkable
I need to map the full black floor path. Let me look at the maze more carefully, especially the left side where the player starts. The player is at [8,14]-[12,18], and below it there's a black area.
Looking at the ASCII:
Row 8: BbbbbbBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBWWWWWWW
Row 9: BbbbbbBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBRRRBBWWWWWWW
Row 10: BbbBbbBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBRRRBBWWWWWWW
Row 11: BbbbbbBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBRRRBBWWWWWWW
Row 12: BbbbbbBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBRBBBWWWWWWW
Row 13: BBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBRBBBWWWWWWW
Row 14: BBBBBBBWWWWWBBBBBBBWWWWWWWBRBWWWWWWWWW
So rows 8-13 have black at cols 13-37 (approximately). The player can move DOWN through this area.
Wait, but earlier when I tried DOWN from [8,20]-[12,24], it was blocked. That's because the white corridor at rows 14-18, cols 20-24 blocked the path.
But from the starting position [8,14]-[12,18], if I move DOWN, the player would go to [14,14]-[18,18]. Let me check what's at rows 14-18, cols 14-18:
Row 14: BBBBBBBWWWWWBBBBBBBWWWWWWWBRBWWWWWWWWW
Cols 14-18: B B B B B (black)
Row 15-18: similar
So the black area at cols 13-19 extends from rows 14-18. The player can move DOWN through this area!
Let me trace the full path:
1. Start: [8,14]-[12,18]
2. DOWN: [14,14]-[18,18] (black area at cols 13-19)
3. DOWN: [20,14]-[24,18] (black area at cols 13-31)
4. DOWN: [26,14]-[30,18] (black area at cols 13-19)
5. DOWN: [32,14]-[36,18] (black area at cols 13-19)
6. DOWN: [38,14]-[42,18] (need to check)
Let me check rows 38-42:
Row 38: BBBBBBBWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWW
Row 39: BBRRRRBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBRBWWWWWWWWW
Row 40: BRRRRRRRRRRRRRRRRRRRRRRRRRRRBWWWWWWWWW
Row 41: BBRRRRBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBWWWWWWWWW
Row 42: BRRRRRBWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWW
Row 38: B(13-19) W(20-50)
Row 39: B(13) B(14) R(15-18) B(19-38) R(39) B(40) W(41-50)
Row 40: B(13) R(14-39) B(40) W(41-50)
Row 41: B(13) B(14) R(15-18) B(19-40) W(41-50)
Row 42: B(13) R(14-18) B(19) W(20-50)
So at rows 39-42, there's red (R) at cols 14-18 (approximately). This would block the player from moving DOWN through cols 14-18.
Hmm, so the path down the left side is blocked by red at rows 39-42. Let me think about this differently.
Maybe the red bar is not a wall but a movable obstacle or a key. Or maybe the player needs to go through a different path.
Let me reconsider the maze layout. The white corridors form a specific pattern:
- Rows 14-18: white at cols 20-24 and 32-38
- Rows 19-25: white at cols 32-38
- Rows 26-38: white at cols 20-38
- Rows 42-47: white at cols 20-50
The black "floor" path is:
- Left column: cols 13-19 (rows 14-38)
- Middle gap: cols 25-31 (rows 14-18)
- Right gap: cols 39-41 (rows 14-38)
- Bottom wide: cols 13-39 (rows 39-41)
- Bottom left: cols 13-19 (rows 42-47)
- Goal room: cols 42-50 (rows 48-55)
The red bar is at cols 14-18 (rows 39-42) and cols 38-40 (rows 9-13).
So the path from top to bottom:
1. Start at [8,14]-[12,18]
2. Move DOWN through left column (cols 13-19)
3. At rows 39-42, red blocks the left column
4. Need to move RIGHT to the right gap (cols 39-41) before reaching rows 39-42
5. Continue DOWN through right gap
6. At the bottom, reach the goal room
But wait, the right gap is at cols 39-41, which is only 3 columns wide. The player is 5 columns wide. So the player can't fit through the right gap.
Hmm, let me reconsider. Maybe the player can move through the red bar? Or maybe the red bar is not a wall.
Let me try moving DOWN from the starting position to see if the player can move through the black area.
Actually wait, I need to reconsider the player size. The player is a 5x5 square with 24 pixels (not 25). This means one cell is different (the black dot in the center). Let me check the boundary:
Player: bbox=[8,14]-[12,18]
That's rows 8-12 (5 rows) and cols 14-18 (5 cols). So it's a 5x5 square.
When the player moves, it shifts by 6 cells. So from [8,14]-[12,18], RIGHT moves to [8,20]-[12,24].
Now, for the player to move DOWN from [8,14]-[12,18], it would go to [14,14]-[18,18]. The cells at rows 14-18, cols 14-18 need to be walkable (black).
Row 14: BBBBBBBWWWWWBBBBBBBWWWWWWWBRBWWWWWWWWW
Cols 14-18: B B B B B (black) - OK
Row 15-18: similar - OK
So the player can move DOWN from the starting position. Let me try it.
But wait, I already tried DOWN from [8,20]-[12,24] and it was blocked. That's because rows 14-18, cols 20-24 are white. But from [8,14]-[12,18], rows 14-18, cols 14-18 are black. So DOWN should work from the starting position.
Let me try DOWN from the current position [8,14]-[12,18].

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model updated: SPACE resets player to starting position [8,14]-[12,18]. Player is back at start. DOWN was blocked from [8,20]-[12,24] because white corridor at cols 20-24 blocks it, but from [8,14]-[12,18], the black area at cols 14-18 extends down through rows 14-18.
Plan: Move DOWN from starting position through the left black corridor. Then map the full path to the goal.
